coredata.dc:description: Biomass aerogel has broad application prospects in eradicating water pollution. However, conventional preparation methods are limited by high energy consumption, which deviates from the original intention of materials for environmental remediation. In this work, a biomass aerogel with “brick–mortar-binder” structure prepared at ambient drying is proposed firstly for the determination and adsorption of Cr(VI). This biomass aerogel consists of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers grafted with CDs serving as “brick”, flexible 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy −oxidized cellulose fibers and carboxymethyl chitosan as “mortar”, and (4, 6-dimethoxy[1.3.5]triazin-2-yl)-4-methylmorpholinium chloride hydrate (DMTMM) functioning as “binder”. The gel and crosslinking of TOCNFs and CMC are initiated by DMTMM. This method produces solid hydrogels that can resist capillary force during environmental volatilization, and become aerogels through freezing, solvent thawing and exchange, and drying at room temperature. Grafting CDs onto electrospun PAN nanofibers not only provides fluorescent signal and adsorption sites, but also prevents CDs from falling off. Meanwhile, nanofibers are introduced into aerogel, which enhances mechanical strength and boosts elasticity of biomass aerogel by 6 times (from 17.60 to 98.85 KPa). This ambient-dried biomass aerogel shows excellent sensitivity and selectivity to Cr(VI), possessing a detection limit as low as 1.12 μM and a linear range of 5–50 μM. It also has an adsorption capacity of 166.25 mg/g for Cr(VI) at room temperature and pH of 2, conforming to pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics and Langmuir models. The mechanism of detecting and adsorbing Cr(VI) by biomass aerogel is clarified. This work presents an environmentally friendly, sustainable and low-energy method for constructing functional biomass aerogels that can be utilized to detect and adsorb heavy metal ions, thereby expanding the application of ambient-dried aerogels in environmental protection.

head.ce:title: Ambient-dried biomass 3D porous aerogel with “brick-mortar-binder” structure for the determination and adsorption of Cr(VI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass aerogel has broad application prospects in eradicating water pollution. However, conventional preparation methods are limited by high energy consumption, which deviates from the original intention of materials for environmental remediation. In this work, a biomass aerogel with “brick–mortar-binder” structure prepared at ambient drying is proposed firstly for the determination and adsorption of Cr(VI). This biomass aerogel consists of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers grafted with CDs serving as “brick”, flexible 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy −oxidized cellulose fibers and carboxymethyl chitosan as “mortar”, and (4, 6-dimethoxy[1.3.5]triazin-2-yl)-4-methylmorpholinium chloride hydrate (DMTMM) functioning as “binder”. The gel and crosslinking of TOCNFs and CMC are initiated by DMTMM. This method produces solid hydrogels that can resist capillary force during environmental volatilization, and become aerogels through freezing, solvent thawing and exchange, and drying at room temperature. Grafting CDs onto electrospun PAN nanofibers not only provides fluorescent signal and adsorption sites, but also prevents CDs from falling off. Meanwhile, nanofibers are introduced into aerogel, which enhances mechanical strength and boosts elasticity of biomass aerogel by 6 times (from 17.60 to 98.85 KPa). This ambient-dried biomass aerogel shows excellent sensitivity and selectivity to Cr(VI), possessing a detection limit as low as 1.12 μM and a linear range of 5–50 μM. It also has an adsorption capacity of 166.25 mg/g for Cr(VI) at room temperature and pH of 2, conforming to pseudo-second-order adsorption kinetics and Langmuir models. The mechanism of detecting and adsorbing Cr(VI) by biomass aerogel is clarified. This work presents an environmentally friendly, sustainable and low-energy method for constructing functional biomass aerogels that can be utilized to detect and adsorb heavy metal ions, thereby expanding the application of ambient-dried aerogels in environmental protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous development of economy, the pollution problem is becoming more and more serious, among which the use of heavy metals in industrial production is increasing [1–5]. Heavy metal ions can exert toxicity even at minimal concentration, posing serious environmental pollution and substantial risks to human health as well as to other forms of life [6,7]. Hexavalent chromium Cr(VI), a member of heavy metals, is frequently generated or released into industrial wastewater from sectors like metallurgy and electroplating. Due to its carcinogenic property, non-degradability and tendency to bioaccumulate, Cr(VI) presents a significant danger to aquatic ecosystems and human health [8]. The World Health Organization (WHO) has established a maximum permissible concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water at 0.05 mg/L [9]. Detecting and removing Cr(VI) from industrial wastewater is essential to ensure safety.

Currently, numerous analytical methods have been devised for detecting Cr(VI) [10]. Among these, the fluorescence method has gained prominence in pollutant detection owing to its user-friendly operation, exceptional responsiveness, and high sensitivity [11,12]. For the removal of Cr(VI), diversified methods are used [13–15]. Out of the various types of methods, adsorption stands out due to its remarkable characteristics, including simplicity, high efficiency, and low cost, making it versatile for the removal of Cr(VI) [16,17]. Recently, significant efforts have been undertaken by numerous researchers to construct materials that can efficiently and sensitively detect and remove Cr(VI). For instance, Jia et al. described the employment of lanthanide coordinating polymeric porous microparticles for detecting and adsorbing Cr(VI) [18]. Zhang et al. prepared ionic porous organic framework that possessed dual functions: selective adsorption and sensitive determination of Cr(VI) [19]. Nevertheless, these materials in powder state can easily lead to secondary pollution to the environment, limiting their application in actual wastewater treatment processes.

Biomass-based aerogels such as chitosan and cellulose aerogels, have garnered significant attention for water purification because of their porous structure, self-standing property, exceptional adsorption capacity, biodegradability, biocompatibility, and abundant chemical groups [20–22]. At present, freeze drying and supercritical drying are the traditional methods for preparing aerogels. Both can effectively replace the solvent in the aerogels with air under different conditions, and retain their internal primary structure. Specifically, freeze drying involves the process of transforming solvents from a solid state into a gaseous state at low temperature and low pressure [23]. While supercritical drying extracts solvents under supercritical conditions to maintain structural integrity [24]. Tang et al. described preparation of an amine functionalized cellulose-based aerogel bead by a low temperature freeze-drying method and used for simultaneously and effectively adsorbing, reducing and chelating Cr(VI) [25]. Wu et al. developed a green hybrid aerogel by using a cryo-freezing technique, which yeast was fixed onto chitin nanofibers [26]. Xu et al. prepared an innovative multifunctional aerogel capable of fluorescence monitoring and removal of Cr(VI) through a freeze-drying method [27]. The aforementioned methods typically require specialized equipment and extreme conditions, long processing time and high energy consumption, which limit the large-scale production of biomass-based aerogels in practice [28].

Recently, the ambient drying is a promising candidate to prepare biomass aerogels instead of traditional drying methods. Ambient drying is a commonly employed technique across various fields, such as seawater evaporation, energy storage, and flame retardancy [29,30]. This is owing to the utilization of mild conditions (ambient temperature and pressure), which eliminates the need for specialized drying equipment. However, as far as we know, no biomass aerogels prepared at ambient drying are found in the field of detection-adsorption. Further, the ambient-dried biomass aerogels with excellent mechanical strength are necessary. Hence, there exists an imperative necessity to develop biomass aerogels prepared by ambient drying for Cr(VI) determination and adsorption. This would simplify the preparation process, improve mechanical strength, enhance removal efficiency and detection capabilities, ultimately achieving environmental sustainability.

In this work, biomass 3D porous aerogel with “brick-mortar-binder” biomimetic texture was prepared by ambient drying and applied to determine and adsorb Cr(VI) for the first time. The biomass 3D porous aerogel is composed of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers grafted with CDs as the “brick”, flexible 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPO)-oxidized cellulose fibers (TOCNFs) and carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC) acting as the “mortar”, and (4, 6-dimethoxy[1.3.5]triazin-2-yl)-4-methylmorpholinium chloride hydrate (DMTMM) functioning as the “binder”. More importantly, the grafting of CDs onto PAN nanofibers provides fluorescent signals for Cr(VI) determination and avoids CDs detachment. CMC and TOCNFs significantly enhance the availability of active sites and introduce a greater density of positive charges, thereby facilitating both chemisorption and electrostatic adsorption processes for Cr(VI). The structure, morphology, mechanical properties, detection and adsorption properties of biomass aerogels were systematically studied. This study offers a novel synthesis scheme for creating biomass aerogels designed for monitoring and removing heavy metal ions and lays a foundation for further exploring environmentally friendly and energy-saving materials for environmental remediation in the future.
Materials and instruments were displayed in the Supporting Information (SI).
CDs and 10 % PAN nanofibers were prepared, and the detailed preparation processes were described in the SI.

10 % PAN nanofibers were immersed in distilled water and moved to a homogenizer for the purpose of crushing. Following this, CDs solutions and crushed PAN nanofibers with varied masses (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0 and 1.5 g) were mixed together and stirred magnetically until a uniform solution was got. Then, the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which contained all reactants, was put in an oven and subjected to a temperature of 140 °C for the reaction duration of 5 h. Once hydrothermal reactions were finished, the mixtures were filtered using a vacuum filtration method to obtain the CP. The CP was subjected to magnetic stirring in distilled water for 12 h effectively to eliminating any unreacted substances. During this process, the distilled water was replaced multiple times. Finally, CP samples contained varied contents of crushed PAN nanofibers (0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0 and 1.5 g) were designated as CP-1, CP-2, CP-3, CP-4, CP-5 and CP-6, respectively. These samples were then placed in a refrigerator for future utilization.

Firstly, 0.13 g of as-prepared TOCNFs (detailed shown in SI) and 1.8 g of CMC were mixed into distilled water, and then various amounts of CP were added into the resulting mixture. After thoroughly stirring, 0.09 g of DMTMM was incorporated into the above mixture, and stirred for an additional 30 min. Then, the solution underwent crosslinking/gelation at a temperature of 45 °C for 3 h, followed by freezing at −20 °C for a duration of 24 h to obtain CP/CMC/TOCNF hydrogels. The hydrogels were then thawed in ethanol. To guarantee the thorough elimination of ethanol, the hydrogels underwent a solvent exchange process with acetone for three or more consecutive times, with each exchange lasting for 4 h. Finally, the CPCTA samples were obtained after drying under ambient conditions. CPCTA with different amounts of CP-3 (0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 and 0.5 g) were named as CTA, CPCTA-1, CPCTA-2, CPCTA-3, CPCTA-4 and CPCTA-5, respectively.

To prepare FD-CPCTA, CP/CMC/TOCNF hydrogels were prepared firstly. The synthesis of CP/CMC/TOCNF hydrogels was identical to that used to prepare CPCTA-3, except that the drying method of hydrogels was different. Specifically, FD-CPCTA was prepared by freeze-drying the hydrogels at − 60 °C utilizing a lyophilizer for a duration of 2 days.

The prepared CPCTA-3 was cut into dimensions of 1 cm × 1 cm. To ascertain the detection sensitivity of CPCTA-3 to Cr(VI), CPCTA-3 was immersed in Cr(VI) solutions (10 mL) containing varying contents (0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100, 200, 300 and 500 μM). To evaluate the selectivity towards Cr(VI), CPCTA-3 was placed into different ion solutions (pH = 2, 10 mL, 1000 μM) and allowed to soak for 5 min. Different ions such as Cr(VI), Ni(II), Mg(II), Pb(II), Fe(III), Fe(II), Co(II), Cu(II), Cd(II) and Al(III) were included. The experiments were carried out at room temperature. The physical photos and fluorescence spectra of CPCTA-3 were recorded under UV light excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) in real samples by CPCTA-3
ce:section.ce:para: In order to showcase broad applicability of CPCTA-3, water samples were collected from diverse locations. Initially, samples from South Lake (Changchun, China) and Songhua River (Jilin, China) underwent centrifugation followed by filtration utilizing syringe filters. Subsequently, CPCTA-3 was utilized to detect water samples which were spiked with varying contents of Cr(VI). Specifically, South Lake water samples containing 0, 15 and 30 μM Cr(VI) were designated as SL-0, SL-1, and SL-2, respectively. Songhua River water samples containing 0, 15 and 30 μM of Cr(VI) were labeled as SR-0, SR-1, and SR-2. The experiments were conducted at 25 °C.

Various factors such as CPCTA-3 dosage, time, and initial Cr(VI) contents on its adsorption were researched. Given that most Cr(VI)-containing wastewaters were highly acidic under actual conditions, a pH level was adjusted to 2 for the purpose of Cr(VI) removal in subsequent research. At pH = 2, Cr(VI) ions mainly existed in the form of HCrO4 - and Cr2O7 2− [31]. Initially, to assess the impact of dosage, varying amounts of CPCTA-3, ranging from 0.01 to 0.05 g, were mixed with separate aliquots of Cr(VI) solutions (100 mg/L, 20 mL). The effect of time on the adsorption efficiency of Cr(VI) was investigated. Multiple samples of CPCTA-3 (0.01 g) were introduced into Cr(VI) solutions (100 mg/L, 20 mL) with a pH adjusted to 2, and aliquots of solutions (5 mL) were periodically extracted from the solutions at different time intervals. Additionally, an evaluation was carried out to explore the impact of initial Cr(VI) contents on adsorption efficiency. For this purpose, CPCTA-3 (0.01 g) was introduced in Cr(VI) solutions (20 mL) with varying initial contents (10, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300 to 500 mg/L).

For recyclability, after adsorbing Cr(VI), the adsorbent was immersed in a 0.1 mol/L NaOH solution and agitated for 12 h. Following this, the adsorbent was meticulously rinsed with distilled water until it reached a neutral pH, allowing it to be reused for subsequent adsorption cycles. The adsorption–desorption process was repeated five times. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

Cr(VI) contents in solution were determined after CPCTA-3 was taken out. The adsorption capacity (qe) of CPCTA-3 to Cr(VI) is quantified using Equation (Eq. (1)):

In the Eq. (1), V (L) is the volume of Cr(VI) solution, m (g) stands for the weight of CPCTA-3, and C0 and Ce (mg/L) denote Cr(VI) contents at the initial and equilibrium time (min), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design mechanism of the ambient-dried biomass 3D porous CPCTA
ce:section.ce:para: The process for the preparation of the ambient-dried biomass 3D porous CPCTA with fluorescent detection and adsorption is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Inspired by “brick-mortar-binder” biomimetic texture, the ambient-dried biomass 3D porous CPCTA comprises of PAN nanofibers grafted with CDs as the “brick”, flexible TOCNFs and CMC as the “mortar”, and DMTMM as the “binder”. Among them, PAN nanofibers are prepared by electrospinning. To provide fluorescent signal for Cr(VI) detection, CDs are chosen and fabricated by hydrothermal reaction. Further, CDs with amino and carboxyl groups offer numerous binding sites, which are utilized for effectively adsorbing. To prevent the CDs from falling off, CDs are grafted onto 10 % PAN nanofibers to form CP by hydrothermal method. To acquire biomass 3D porous CPCTA, TOCNFs, CMC and DMTMM are used to promote the formation of the framework. Because the molecular chains of TOCNFs and CMC contain abundant amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, and CP forms a complete and strong interpenetrating network with the skeleton of TOCNFs and CMC through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. CP/CMC/TOCNF hydrogel is obtained by using a crosslinking agent DMTMM. Water in hydrogel is substituted with low surface tension of acetone using a straightforward solvent displacement approach. Finally, the ambient-dried CPCTA is obtained by removing the acetone at 25 °C under ambient pressure.

As shown in Fig. 1b(I), two CPCTA-3 can delicately stand at a single Lily petal without distortion, which demonstrates low density and ultralight feature of CPCTA-3 (0.0234 g/cm3). Fig. 1b(II) confirms the reproducibility of CPCTA-3 prepared by ambient drying. As demonstrated in Fig. 1c, ambient-dried CPCTA-3 can exhibit excellent performances across multiple dimensions (Adsorption capacity, fluorescence intensity, shrinkage, limit of detection and compressive properties). Moreover, the preparation of ambient-dried CPCTA-3 does not need specific special environment, thereby further decreasing energy consumption and costs. This implies its potential for advancement into a novel biomass aerogel suitable for detection and adsorption.

Fig. 2 a depicts FTIR spectra of PAN nanofibers, CP-3, CTA and CPCTA-3. FTIR spectrum of PAN nanofibers shows a distinctive absorption band centered at a wavelength of 2243 cm−1 due to CN. The existence of band at 2243 cm−1 are indicative of the presence of PAN in CP-3 and CPCTA-3 [32]. Additionally, the decrease in 2243 cm−1 also indicates the formation of abundant amides following the hydrolysis of CN. For CP-3, CTA and CPCTA-3, a new band emerges at a wavelength of 1647 cm−1 and is assigned to CONH stretching vibration mode. It is demonstrated that a reaction occurs between CN of PAN nanofibers and NH2 of CDs during the synthesis of CP-3 [33]. For CTA and CPCTA-3, the stretching vibrations of NH2 and OH groups are responsible for characteristic peaks found at 3380–3520 cm−1 [34,35]. The bands found between 3000 and 2800 cm−1 belong to CH stretching vibrations [36]. Three distinct peaks locate at 1647, 1557 and 1310 cm−1 for amides I, II and III bands, respectively [37]. Additionally, COOH peak located at 1600 cm−1 is overlapped by peaks at 1647 and 1557 cm−1 [38]. In summary, the presences of the new amide peak and the CN peak offer strong evidence for the inclusion of CP-3 in CPCTA-3 and the formation of crosslinking between COOH and NH2 groups. XPS analysis further confirms that CPCTA-3 enriches with functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, rendering it suitable for sensing and removing Cr(VI) (Fig. S1).

In order to confirm that ambient-drying technology can be used as an alternative to freeze-drying for fabricating aerogel, two aerogels, namely CPCTA-3 (prepared via ambient drying) and FD-CPCTA-3 (prepared via freeze-drying) are prepared. As shown in Fig. 2b, in terms of appearance, there is little difference between CPCTA-3 and FD-CPCTA-3. Moreover, the diameter of CPCTA-3 is approximately 27.5 mm, which is almost the same as that of FD-CPCTA-3 (27.6 mm). This indicates the shrinkage rate of CPCTA-3 (prepared via ambient drying) is very small, which is comparable to that of FD-CPCTA-3 (obtained by freeze-drying). Microscopically, both CPCTA-3 and FD-CPCTA-3 exhibit a porous 3D network structure. The difference is that FD-CPCTA-3 exhibits a honeycomb structure, as displayed in Fig. 2c, while CPCTA-3 possesses parallel channels (Fig. 2d). These parallel channels in the CPCTA-3 are similar to plant ducts and are beneficial for the transportation of liquid. The CPCTA-3 has a larger specific surface area (Fig. S2), which helps to enhance its adsorption capacity.

The structural evolution during the ambient-drying process is summarized in Fig. 3 . According to the Young-Laplace equation, preventing pore collapse during the ambient-drying process involves two critical factors: increasing the strength of the gel skeleton and reducing the surface tension [39]. Regarding the strength of the gel skeleton, the long chains of CP wrap the skeleton through physical entanglement and hydrogen bonding, forming a structure similar to climbing plants. This structure drastically enhances the strength of the skeleton, effectively preventing the aerogel from structural collapse during solvent evaporation process. In the aspect of the surface tension, water is replaced by acetone with low surface tension coefficient, which further prevents the aerogel from structural collapse during acetone evaporation process at ambient pressure.

The mechanical properties of pure CTA without/with adding CP-3 after 1, 3, and 5 loading and unloading cycles are studied. As presented in Fig. 4 a, in comparison to pure CTA, the introduction of CP-3 notably boosts the mechanical elasticity of aerogels. Further, with increasing CP-3 amount, the mechanical elasticity of the CPCTA first increases and then decreases and is much larger than that of CTA. When the CP-3 content is 0.2 g, the compressive stress of CPCTA-3 is as high as 98.85 KPa. When the CP-3 content increases to 0.5 g, the compressive stress of CPCTA-5 decreases to 67.90 KPa. It can be found from Fig. S3 that CPCTA-3 achieves the best mechanical properties, which is nearly 6 times stronger than CTA. Meanwhile, CPCTA-3 exhibits shape recovery properties in wet state, suggesting its flexibility for adapting to complex water environments (Fig. S4, Movie S1, SI).

In addition, CPCTA-3 can still recover to 83 % of its original level after 20 loading–unloading cycles (Fig. 4b), indicating that it has good mechanical elasticity and confirming that the addition of CP-3 improves the overall skeleton strength. To explore the influence of CP-3 addition on mechanical strength of aerogel, the microscopic pore structures in cross sections of aerogel before and after compression are observed. As manifested in Fig. 4c, before compression, aerogels all show a similar parallel channel structure. Specially, the surface of CTA is relatively smooth (Fig. 4c I). Upon incorporating appropriate CP-3, the surface of CPCTA-3 transforms into a distinctly roughened texture, the pore size distribution is relatively uniform, and the aerogel still maintains an intact structure (Fig. 4c II). When CP-3 is added excessively, it can be clearly observed that the structure of the CPCTA-5 is destroyed, and the lamellar structure is pierced by nanofibers (Fig. 4c III). After compression, aerogels with different CP-3 additions show different states. The overall structure of CTA collapses severely after being compressed (Fig. S5a), making it impossible to maintain the original pore size structure. However, the CPCTA-3 still maintains its original morphology after compression, and the structure does not change significantly (Fig. S5b). When the addition of CP-3 is excessive, although the original structure of CPCTA-5 can still be observed after compression, some structures still collapse (Fig. S5c). This may be due to the addition of excessive CP-3 destroying the basic skeleton of CMC/TOCNF (Fig. 4c). This structural damage impacts the overall integrity of material, rendering it more susceptible to structural instability and fracture when exposed to stress. In conclusion, we reasonably speculate that CP-3 contributes to improving mechanical behavior by mechanical interlocking and chemical bonding within the aerogel matrix (Fig. 4d).

The stability of the aerogel in aqueous solution is important for practical applications. As shown in Fig. S6-S7 and Video S2, after soaking the CPCTA-3 in an aqueous solution for a period of time, it is found that the structure of CPCTA-3 remains intact. Under UV irradiation, CPCTA-3 immersed in the solution emits light, while the solution does not, indicating that CDs cannot leak from CPCTA-3. The mass loss rate of CPCTA-3 before and after soaking-drying in water is only 2.7 %. These demonstrate CPCTA-3 has excellent stability under water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and determination of Cr(VI) by CPCTA
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra of CDs are recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs aqueous suspension reveals two distinctive peaks situated at 240 and 360 nm. These peaks are associated with π-π* transition arising from aromatic ring and n-π* transition originating from carbonyl group present in amino-modified CDs, respectively [40]. By using 450 nm as monitoring wavelength, the peak at 310 nm can be observed. When the CDs are excited at 310 nm, a distinct emission peak at 450 nm is observable in the blue dashed line of fluorescence emission spectrum. The corresponding picture, exhibited in the inset of Fig. 5a, displays CDs aqueous solution remains fully transparent in sunlight, but CDs exhibit blue light emission when exposed to a UV lamp. As demonstrated in Fig. 5b, the CDs exhibit a slight excitation-dependent fluorescence emission characteristics, this means that the CDs are not entirely graphitized, and the majority of the observed fluorescence arises from the recombination of energy levels within surface functional groups, instead of energy level transitions of carbonized cores [41].

To detect Cr(VI), fluorescent CP formed by grafting CDs onto PAN nanofibers is added into the aerogel. To explore the best luminescent aerogel, fluorescence spectra of the prepared nanofibers (CP-1, CP-2, CP-3, CP-4, CP-5, and CP-6) are illustrated in Fig. S8a. All these nanofibers exhibit fluorescence, but their fluorescent intensities are different. CP-3 exhibits the strongest fluorescence performance. Therefore, CP-3 is added for the construction of CPCTA. To investigate the different amounts of CP-3 added on the aerogels, five biomass aerogels (CPCTA-1, CPCTA-2, CPCTA-3, CPCTA-4, and CPCTA-5) are prepared. Fig. 5c displays the appearance and fluorescent properties of biomass aerogels, revealing that they all possess prominent blue fluorescence. Combining with Fig. S8b, CPCTA-3 emits the brightest fluorescence and has the maximum fluorescent quantum yield of 5.62 %. To verify the uniformity of the fluorescence distribution, CPCTA-3 is vertically and horizontally cut to facilitate a comprehensive cross-sectional analysis. It can be found from Fig. 5d that all cross-sections of CPCTA-3 exhibit identical fluorescence as observed on its surface, meaning that CP-3 uniformly exists from the external to the internal of CPCTA-3. Therefore, CPCTA-3 is used to detect Cr(VI).

Fig. 5e shows the fluorescence emission spectra of CPCTA-3 with excitation wavelengths of 300–380 nm. It can be found that compared to fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs (Fig. 5b), CPCTA-3 exhibits a pronounced dependence on excitation wavelength, with its optimal emission peak changing from 450 nm to 440 nm (Fig. S8c). This occurrence is largely due to the immobilized CDs within CPCTA-3. The decrease in the dipole moment of the excited state results in a reduction of the energy loss, ultimately leading to a blue shift in fluorescence emission [42,43]. Further, the fluorescence emission peak of CPCTA-3 is broader in comparison to that of CDs solution (Fig. S8c). This is because the interactions among TOCNFs, CMC and CDs, which include processes such as cross-linking, physical entanglement and electrostatic interaction. These interactions enrich the surface states of CDs, consequently broadening the emission peak of CPCTA-3 [44].

Fluorescence sensing abilities of CPCTA-3 for Cr(VI) are explored. It can be observed from Fig. 6 a that the fluorescent intensities of CPCTA-3 progressively decrease as increasing Cr(VI) concentration (0–500 μM). From Fig. 6b, the fluorescence variation ratio (I/I0) of CPCTA-3 varies linearly when the Cr(VI) concentration is 5–50 μM (I0 and I are the fluorescent intensity located at 440 nm without and with Cr(VI) addition, respectively). As illustrated in Fig. 6b, under 365 nm-UV illumination, significant fluorescence changes are observed in CPCTA-3 when exposed to different Cr(VI) concentrations. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated using the formula LOD = 3σ/S, with S being the slope of the calibration curve and σ representing the standard deviation derived from five measurements of blank samples. Hence, LOD is calculated to be 1.12 μM, which is below the Cr(VI) effluent standard declared by WHO and comparable to the reported materials (Table S1).

In order to verify the detection selectivity, common metal ions (Cr(VI), Pb(II), Ni(II), Mg(II), Fe(III), Fe(II), Co(II), Cu(II), Cd(II) and Al(III)) frequently found in various industrial wastewaters are selected to investigate their influence on the fluorescence of CPCTA-3. As shown in Fig. 6c, the fluorescence intensity of CPCTA-3 is significantly quenched by Cr(VI), whereas other metal ions only cause minor quenching. This is attributed to the superior electron-capturing capability of Cr(VI), enabling a greater transfer of electrons from CPCTA-3 towards Cr(VI), causing a noticeable quenching effect [31].

To evaluate practical applicability of CPCTA-3, the standard addition method is used to ascertain Cr(VI) concentrations in actual water samples. As presented in Fig. 6d, a gradual increase in the added Cr(VI) concentration corresponds with a notable decline in the fluorescence intensity of CPCTA-3. The recovery rates of CPCTA-3 fall within the range of 95.95 % to 102.63 %, with relative standard deviation (RSD) varying between 0.09 % and 2.58 %, as shown in Table 1 . These results are consistent with the ICP-MS results confirm that CPCTA-3 is reliable for detecting Cr(VI) in actual water samples.

Adsorbing Cr(VI) by CPCTA-3 is visualized in Fig. 7 a. One piece of CPCTA-3 (with a diameter of 6 cm and a height of 0.5 cm) is immersed into Cr(VI) solution. Over time, the color of solution transitions from yellow to colorless, revealing that the 3D porous fluorescent CPCTA-3 possesses outstanding sorption capabilities for Cr(VI).

In order to avoid unnecessary waste caused by excessive addition of adsorbents, the effect of different dosages of CPCTA-3 on adsorption capacity of Cr(VI) is studied. As depicted in Fig. S9, with increasing the dosages of CPCTA-3, the adsorption capacity is on the decrease. This is because the adsorption capacity is inversely proportional to the dosage of adsorbent. So, 0.01 g of CPCTA-3 is fixed to conduct the follow-up studies.

Adsorption is researched as a function of contact time to study the adsorption kinetics of Cr(VI) onto CPCTA-3. As presented in Fig. 7b, the adsorption capacity of CPCTA-3 rapidly increases within the first 60 min, reaching 70 % of its maximum capacity, and then slowly reaches saturation. Pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) models (Eq. S1-S2) are employed to study adsorption mechanism [45,46]. The fitting curves of both models for CPCTA-3 are displayed in Fig. 7b, along with the relevant parameters listed in Table S2. Higher R2 of PSO and closer alignment of its theoretical value of qe to the experimental value indicate that the PSO kinetic model is suitable for explaining the adsorption process of Cr(VI) by CPCTA-3, further implying a chemical adsorption process.

Furthermore, the intra-particle diffusion model (Eq. S3) is applied to elucidate the diffusion mechanism [47]. As illustrated in Fig. 7c, the presence of three linear regions indicates that there is more than one mechanism involved in the overall adsorption process. The first stage, which exhibits the highest diffusion rate, is a result of the immediate migration of Cr(VI) from the aqueous solution to outer surface of CPCTA-3. As the process progresses, a noticeable deceleration in the adsorption rate indicates that pore or intraparticle diffusion emerges as the predominant rate-controlling mechanism. The final linear segment signifies the equilibrium adsorption stage. Notably, none of these lines intersect with the coordinate origin, suggesting that adsorbing Cr(VI) by CPCTA-3 involves pore diffusion and chemical adsorption processes [48,49].

Fig. 7d-h shows the adsorption isotherms of Cr(VI) on CPCTA-3. Langmuir, Freundlich, Jovanovich, Halsey, Temkin and Dubinin-Radushkevich (D-R) (Eq. S4-S10) of CPCTA-3 are investigated [50–54]. It can be seen from Fig. 7d and Table S3 that the adsorption isotherm for CPCTA-3 is best fitted with the Langmuir model, with the highest R2 (0.98), revealing adsorbing Cr(VI) on CPCTA-3 occurs in homogeneous monolayer [31]. The correlation coefficient in the Temkin model and the E value (>16 kJ/mol) in the D-R model indicate that the adsorption process is mainly chemical adsorption [55]. As shown in Table S4, qmax of CPCTA-3 for Cr(VI) is 166.25 mg/g, which is comparable to or even superior to that of most biomass and its derivatives adsorbents recently reported in the literatures. This means that the ambient-dried biomass CPCTA-3 stands out as an exceptional adsorbent for the effective elimination of Cr(VI) and the ambient drying method become a new green method for preparing aerogel adsorbents.

For actual applications, the reuse of adsorbents not only helps to reduce costs and save resources, but also has significant implications for environmental protection and sustainable development. As presented in Fig. 7i, even after undergoing 5 cycles of adsorption/desorption, the adsorption efficiency of CPCTA-3 remains above 89.3 %. This negligible change may result from the consumption of functional groups like hydroxyl group by continuously providing electrons to reduce Cr(VI), thus leading to a slight decrement in the reduction capability of CPCTA-3. In addition, Cr(III) complexes formed after adsorption remain incompletely removable from CPCTA-3, resulting in the active sites (such as amino groups) of CPCTA-3 being partially occupied. Nevertheless, the CPCTA-3 is still considered to be a highly efficient and reusable adsorbent, ensuring its sustainability in water treatment and preventing excessive expenditure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for Cr(VI) sensing and adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the sensing and adsorption mechanism of CPCTA-3 on Cr(VI), a series of spectral analyses are performed. As manifested in Fig. 8 a, UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) exhibits a band between 300 and 500 nm, which overlaps with the optimal excitation and emission peaks of CPCTA-3 presented in the fluorescence spectra, suggesting that fluorescence quenching may be ascribed to the inner filter effect (IFE) [56–58]. To validate this detection mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of CPCTA-3 before and after exposure to Cr(VI) are examined. Fig. S10 reveals that the average fluorescence lifetime of CPCTA-3 is 4.15 ns, and that of CPCTA-3/Cr(VI) is 4.14 ns, proving evidence for the occurrence of IFE. Based on IFE, UV–Vis absorption spectra of other metal ions rarely overlap or even do not overlap the excitation and emission spectra of CPCTA-3 (Fig. S11), verifying that CPCTA-3 has excellent selectivity for Cr(VI).

FTIR spectra of CPCTA-3 before and after adsorbing Cr(VI) are depicted in Fig. 8b. The typical bands at 941 and 754 cm−1 are associated with asymmetric Cr-O stretching and Cr-O-Cr bridge vibrations, respectively [59–61]. This result validates the adsorption of Cr(VI) on CPCTA-3. Additionally, adsorbing Cr(VI) by CPCTA-3 is further verified by the elemental distribution in EDS mappings (Fig. S11). XPS analyses of CPCTA-3 without and with Cr(VI) adsorption are further utilized to enhance comprehension of adsorption mechanism. XPS spectrum of CPCTA-3 after Cr(VI) uptake (Fig. 8c) reveals distinct Cr 2p peaks, providing clear evidence of the effective capture and immobilization of Cr(VI) by CPCTA-3. To be precise, there are four distinct peaks located around 587.0, 585.4, 577.8 and 575.7 eV (Fig. 8d). The peaks at 585.4 and 575.7 eV are indicative of the presence of Cr(III), whereas those at 587.0 and 577.8 eV are characteristic of Cr(VI) [62,63]. These peaks of Cr after adsorption move to the low energy direction, compared with XPS spectra of Cr in potassium dichromate [64,65]. This observation suggests that some Cr(VI) ions undergo reduction to Cr(III), with the resulting Cr(III) being fixed on CPCTA-3. The residual Cr(VI) ions are primarily adsorbed onto CPCTA-3 through electrostatic interactions.

Fig. 8e illustrates the high-resolution XPS spectra of O 1 s of CPCTA-3 without and with Cr(VI) adsorption. Initially, prior to Cr(VI) adsorption, bands at 531.4 and 530.1 eV are attributed to C=O and C-O, respectively. Upon Cr(VI) adsorption, very small shifts are observed in these two bands, indicating that Cr(VI) binds to the oxygen atoms of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. Additionally, high resolution XPS spectra of N 1 s in Fig. 8f reveal peaks at 397.9 and 400.0 eV corresponding to –NH and –NH2, respectively. After Cr(VI) adsorption, a slight shift of these peaks towards higher binding energies is observed for both –NH and –NH2, indicating a robust interaction occurs between the nitrogen functional groups and Cr species.

Therefore, based on these results, the mechanism of CPCTA-3 for the determination and adsorption of Cr(VI) can be reasonably inferred as shown in Fig. 9 . CDs are anchored on PAN nanofibers and encapsulated in the CPCTA-3 as the fluorescent source. When the CPCTA-3 is immersed in the Cr(VI) solution, Cr(VI) moves quickly from the surface of the CPCTA-3 to the interior and the fluorescence of CPCTA-3 is quenched as a result of IFE, thereby generating fluorescence quenching.

The exceptional adsorption capacity of CPCTA-3 for Cr(VI) is attributed to several synergistic effects. Firstly, the unique 3D porous structure creates pathways for ion transportation, allowing for easy diffusion of Cr(VI) from outside to inside of CPCTA-3, enabling effective surface and bulk adsorption. Next, the strong cross-linking between CP, CMC, and TOCNFs endows CPCTA-3 with outstanding chemical stability, which enables it to effectively and reliably adsorb Cr(VI). Further, the abundance of functional groups (amino groups, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups) available on the CPCTA-3 surface offers numerous adsorption sites for Cr(VI). Under acidic conditions, the abundant protonated functional groups on CPCTA-3 enable rapid immobilization of negatively charged Cr(VI) ions (HCrO4 − and Cr2O7 2−) on the surface of CPCTA-3 through electrostatic interactions. Additionally, a portion of Cr(VI) ions undergoes reduction to less harmful Cr(III), with the assistance of electron donors like hydroxyl groups. The resulting Cr(III) is completely fixed on the CPCTA-3 through coordination with deprotonated amino groups, without being released back into the aqueous environment [66]. The possible adsorption mechanism is proposed as follows [67,68]:
(2) R-COOH + R-OH + R-NH2 + H+→R-NH3 + + R-OH2 + + R-COOH2 +
(3) R-NH3 + + HCrO4 − + Cr2O7 2−→ R-NH3 +⋅⋅⋅HCrO4 − + R-NH3 +⋅⋅⋅Cr2O7 2−
(4) R-OH2 + + R-COOH2 + + HCrO4 − + Cr2O7 2−→ R-OH2 +⋅⋅⋅HCrO4 − + R-COOH2 +⋅⋅⋅Cr2O7 2− + R-COOH2 +⋅⋅⋅HCrO4 − + R-OH2 +⋅⋅⋅Cr2O7 2−
(5) HCrO4 −+7H++3e-→Cr3+ +4H2O
(6) Cr2O7 2−+14H++6e-→2Cr3+ +7H2O
(7) R-NH2 + Cr3+→ R-NH2⋅⋅⋅Cr3+

In conclusion, a 3D porous biomass aerogel (CPCTA-3) is prepared through ambient drying technology for determination and adsorption of Cr(VI) firstly. CPCTA-3 with 3D porous network structure contains rich oxygen-containing groups, amino groups, which is helpful for the determination and adsorption. By introducing CP into the biomass aerogel network, not only the fluorescence signal is introduced, but also mechanical properties are significantly improved. Compared with the pure aerogel without CP, the mechanical strength of CPCTA-3 is increased by 6 times and the strengthening mechanism is studied in detail. The CPCTA-3 has high sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of Cr(VI) with a linear range of 5–50 μM and a detection limit of 1.12 μM. It is mainly owing to IFE between CDs and Cr(VI). The adsorption kinetics and isotherm model fitting verify that the adsorption behavior is single-layer chemical adsorption, and the maximum adsorption capacity reaches 166.25 mg/g, which is equivalent to the adsorption of most reported biomass-based aerogels prepared by freeze-drying. After 5 cycles of adsorption–desorption, CPCTA-3 still maintains a high adsorption capacity, demonstrating good stability and adsorption properties. Furthermore, this ambient-dried biomass aerogel has important application prospects in environmental protection, energy conservation, adsorption, detection and large-scale preparation of biomass aerogel.
